rulings.Case Title: JOE DRUMMOND et al VS. FRANCESCA ASSOCIATION, A CALIFORNIA MUTUAL BENEFIT et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Quash Subpoenas Purpostedly Served On Canyon Pacific Management Co., Inc. And Eugene Burger Management Corporation
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, January 16, 2015, Line 2, RESPONDENT FRANCESCA ASSOCIATION, Motion To Quash Subpoenas Purportedly Served On Canyon Pacific Management Co., Inc. And Eugene Burger Management Corporation. Motion to Quash is denied except subpoenaed parties/defendant need not produce documents subject to claims of privilege pursuant to the Davis-Stirling Act, California Civil Code section 5215, or any other applicable claim of privilege. As to documents withheld based a claim of privilege, subpoenaed parties/defendant shall provide a privilege log identifying the nature of the document, writer, recipient, date of writing and facts supporting the basis for the privilege claim. Documents responsive to the subpoenas, a privilege log, and/or declarations of non-possession of documents shall be served within ten days of the date of this Order. Plaintiff's request for sanctions is denied. The court will not provide a court reporter for this discovery hearing. If any party wishes a reporter, the parties must meet and confer and agree on a single reporter. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: ZIKL INC. VS. DAMON PACE
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioner Zikl Inc. Hearing On Petition To Confirm Arbitration Award.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, January 16, 2015, Line 14, Petitioner Zikl Inc. Hearing On Petition To Confirm Arbitration Award. The petition to confirm the arbitration award is granted. Respondent was served with the petition to confirm on November 7, 2014. Pursuant to CCP 1290.6, respondent had 10 days to file and serve a response. Respondent filed no such response and the allegations of the petition to confirm are deemed admitted. See CCP 1290. Respondent filed a petition to vacate the arbitration award on December 17, 2014. The petition to vacate is disregarded because it is untimely and moot in light of respondent's admissions. Pursuant to CCP 1288, respondent had 100 days from the service of the award to petition the court to vacate the award. The arbitrator served the award on August 8, 2014, and the respondent did not file and serve his petition to vacate within the mandatory deadline. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: THE HOUSING AUTHORITY VS. UNITED ASSOCIATION OF JOURNEYMEN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Petition To Vacate Contractual Arbitration Award
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, January 16, 2015, Line 13, PETITIONER THE HOUSING AUTHORITY OF THE CITY AND COUNTY Hearing On Petition To Vacate Contractual Arbitration Award. The Petition to Vacate is granted because the award exceeds the arbitrators' authority. The MOU pursuant to which the arbitrators derived any authority had expired prior to the allegedly prohibited conduct, and it could not be extended merely by an oral agreement between representatives of each party. (See Gov. Code § 3505.1.) Further, SFHA did not consent to have the arbitrator determine the question of arbitrability on the second grievance. Local 38's own evidence establishes that "the Committee heard the first grievance and agreed to continue the second grievance for two weeks." (Mares Decl. 10.) Finally, because the award does not specify how the remedy was calculated, nor how much of that remedy is related to each grievance, the Court cannot correct the award without affecting the merits of the decision upon the controversy submitted. In light of this ruling, the Cross-Petition to Confirm the Award is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: JAMES COREY BUSCH VS. JAMES BRADFORD et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alt Summ Adj
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, January 16, 2015, Line 4, DEFENDANT JAMES BRADFORD MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication. The motion for summary judgment is denied and the motion for summary adjudication is granted and denied in part. The Court denies summary adjudication as to issues 1 and 4. Defendant fails to show that he did not owe a duty of care to plaintiffs. Admiralty law does not recognize the assumption of the risk doctrine. See Barber v. Sailing, Inc. (1995) 36 Cal.App.4th 558. Defendant's argument that he owed no duty of care to plaintiffs based on the nature of the activity engaged in is a primary assumption of the risk argument. The Court grants summary adjudication as to issues 2, 3, 5, 6, and 7 [seaman status]. It does not appear that defendant is challenging the first element of Chandris, Inc. v. Latsis (1995) 515 U.S. 347, 368-the requirement that an employee "contribute to the function of the vessel or to the accomplishment of its mission." Plaintiffs', however, cannot establish the second element-the requirement of a substantial connection to a vessel in both duration and nature. Plaintiffs' connection to the boat was not substantial because it was recreational in nature. Plaintiffs were not compensated and had regular land-based employment. With regard to duration, Plaintiffs' connection to the boat was not substantial and the temporal connection was inadequate under the 30 percent rule of thumb cited with approval in Chandris, which denies seaman status to "[a] worker who spends less than about 30 percent of his time in the service of a vessel in navigation." 515 U.S. at 371. The instant case is similar to Xanadu Maritime Trust v. Meyer, 21 F.Supp.2d 1104, 1107 (N.D.Cal.1998). There, the court found that the plaintiff's connection to the boat was not substantial in duration or nature, and that he was not a seaman. The plaintiff was engaged in recreational sailing on defendant's boat and was injured while performing a task related to the sail. The plaintiff was one of several people on whom the defendant called to crew the boat, but had no standing arrangement or commitment to crew. The plaintiff had been out on the boat not more than six times in a span of two years. Xanadu explains that the purpose of the Chandris test is to extend seaman status only to those who in their work are regularly exposed to the perils of the sea. The plaintiffs in this case do not meet that test. The Court denies summary adjudication as to issues 8-10 [vicarious liability]. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding Mr. Cahill's employment-related connection to the vessel and the degree of control that defendant exercised over Mr. Cahill. The evidence demonstrates that defendant owned the vessel, paid Mr. Cahill to serve as the helmsman of the vessel, and defendant was at least partially in charge of the vessel. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: ENID SHOOR et al VS. MICHAEL BOZORA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, January 16, 2015, Line 6, DEFENDANT MARK WILLIAMS, RUZZO SCHOLL & MURPHY, SARATOGA FAMILY COUNSELING, INC. INCORRECTLY MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. Granted in part and denied in part. Granted as to the tenth cause of action for unjust enrichment. Defendants met their initial burden by showing that they did not receive compensation from the Dormans. (Undisputed Material Facts ("UMF"), Issue 5, nos. 1-6.) Plaintiffs Clive and Olivia Dorman do not submit evidence identifying a direct benefit that Defendants received at the expense of Plaintiffs. Denied as to the other causes of action. There is a triable issue of material fact as to whether Mark Williams owed the Dormans a fiduciary duty. Plaintiffs present evidence showing that in March 2009 Mark Williams met with the Dormans and instructed them to make an additional investment. (Plaintiffs' Additional Material Facts ("AMF") nos. 12-31.) Thus, even if Williams was not officially hired by the Dormans as their financial advisor, a reasonable finder of fact could find that Williams owed them a fiduciary duty because he voluntarily provided them financial advice and because of his longstanding, close relationship with the family. As to the fourth and sixth causes of action for negligent misrepresentation, Defendants did not meet their initial burden. Showing that the Dormans did not officially hire or compensate Williams does not entirely negate the duty element. As to the fifth cause of action for fraud, Defendants met their initial burden by showing that Plaintiffs cannot establish justifiable reliance. (UMF, Issue 2, nos. 7-14.) In opposition, Plaintiffs raise a triable issue of material fact as to justifiable reliance by providing evidence that Plaintiffs trusted Williams' word over the printed materials, given his longstanding relationship with Enid Shoor. (AMF 12.) As to the eighth cause of action, as discussed above, there is a triable issue of material fact as to whether Williams owed Plaintiffs a fiduciary duty. As to the eleventh cause of action, there is a triable issue of material fact as to whether Williams was selling securities at the March 2009 meeting. Defendants' objections are overruled, as Defendants did not comply with CRC 3.1354(c). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: PERSOLVE, LLC, A LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY, DBA VS. SONIA CORONADO-BROWN et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Default, Vacate Judgment, And For Leave To File Answer
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, January 16, 2015, Line 8, DEFENDANT SONIA CORONADO-BROWN Motion To Set Aside Default, Vacate Judgment, And For Leave To File Answer. Granted. The default and default judgment are set aside. Defendant shall file her proposed answer within 10 days of entry of this order. Defendant submits evidence that she did not respond to the complaint because of "mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect." (CCP sec. 473(b).) Substitute service was effectuated on Defendant's mother, who speaks little English, and mistakenly did not understand the papers and did not give them to her daughter. (Declaration of Norma Coronado; Declaration of Sonia Coronado-Brown, pars., 2, 4.) There is conflicting evidence presented about whether Defendant was informed by Plaintiff about the lawsuit. (Declaration of Dawn Stafseth, pars. 2-4; Declaration of Sonia Coronado-Brown, pars. 2-3.) "Because the law strongly favors trial and disposition on the merits, any doubts in applying section 473 must be resolved in favor of the party seeking relief from default." (Elston v. City of Turlock (1985) 38 Cal.3d 227, 233.) Further, where the party in default moves promptly to seek relief and no prejudice to the opposing party will result from setting aside the default and letting the case go to trial on the merits, "very slight evidence will be required to justify a court in setting aside the default." (Elston v. City of Turlock (1985) 38 Cal.3d 227, 233.) Plaintiff does not establish that it will be prejudiced by setting aside the default and default judgment and permitting the case to proceed on the merits. rulings.Case Title: CHARLES LI VS. THAI MING CHIU et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion (1) To Compel Answers To Form Interrogatories And Special Interrogatories, (2) To Compel Production Of Documents, And (3) For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, January 16, 2015, Line 6, PLAINTIFF CHARLES LI Notice Of Motion And Motion (1) To Compel Answers To Form Interrogatories And Special Interrogatories, (2) To Compel Production Of Documents, And (3) For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Motion is GRANTED. All objections are overruled. A party may make an objection to all or part of a demand but cannot make general objections and repeatedly incorporate the general objections into each response. CCP § 2031.210(a). A motion to compel lies where objections are "too general." CCP § 2030.300(a)(3); see Korea Data Systems Co. Ltd. v. Sup.Ct. (Aamazing Technologies Corp.) (1997) 51 Cal. App. 4th 1513, 1516. Plaintiff shall provide amended, verified, code-compliant responses to the discovery at issue, along with responsive documents, within two weeks of notice of entry of this order. Plaintiff need not provide amended responses where no objection was raised and proper responses were provided. (See Special Interrogatories 11, 14, 17). The request for sanctions is denied. (See Rutter Group Guide, Civil Procedure Before Trial, 8:1237.2; CCP § 2023.040)(failure to cite authority for sanctions in notice of motion). Defendant shall email a proposed order in Word format consistent with this tentative ruling to pcc@manningllp.com by 5pm today. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to pcc@manningllp.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. =302/JPT

rulings.Case Title: JOY B VS. ANDREW S.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Reponses To Defendant Andrew Sauses Request For Production Of Documents By Plaintiff (Set Two) And For Sanctions_1041722
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, January 16, 2015, Line 7, DEFENDANT ANDREW SAUSE Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Reponses To Defendant Andrew Sauses Request For Production Of Documents By Plaintiff (Set Two) And For Sanctions_1041722 Pro Tem Judge Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Motion is GRANTED. The Motion is timely. (See Stimpert Declaration, Exhibit F, p. 3, par. 3). Plaintiff alleges extreme back pain in the Complaint. Plaintiff's counsel intimates that plaintiff suffered physical injuries to her spine from a car accident. (See Stimpert Declaration, Exhibit B, p. 3, par. 6). Plaintiff shall provide an amended, verified, code-compliant response to RFP 22, along with responsive documents, within two weeks of notice of entry of this order. The request for sanctions is GRANTED in the amount of $250. Defendant shall email a proposed order in Word format consistent with this tentative ruling to pcc@manningllp.com by 5pm today. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to pcc@manningllp.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. =302/JPT

rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn For Bifurcation Of Discovery
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, January 16, 2015, Line 10, DEFENDANT COMMUNITY HOUSING PARTNERSHIP'S Mtn For Bifurcation Of Discovery Pro Tem Judge Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Motion is DENIED. Defendant provided no evidence to support its arguments regarding "unnecessary costly and time consuming discovery." Defendant failed to provide any examples of written discovery that plaintiff has sought that would support defendant's arguments. Plaintiff is entitled to discovery regarding common questions of fact and typicality to putative class members. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to pcc@manningllp.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/JPT
